## [00:00 - 03:00]

So the third factor contributing to your ad success is ad format. So what are we talking about with ad format? It's this area here when creating your ad. You've got to decide what the, I guess, creative is. So is it a carousel? Is it a single image? Is it a video? Is it a collection, instant experience? Should you use a video? Should you post a collection? Use an instant experience? Post a carousel of usually images, where there are multiple images together. Maybe even use a lead form or you're collecting leads directly on Facebook. So lots of questions you have to ask yourself. But really, you know, I think we overcomplicate this. So how should we approach ad format? Ultimately, the format should not be your primary concern. I think this is where we often get lost. It's like, oh, I've heard that video is the best to use or imagery is the best to just images. And we get lost in this, especially with organic content. For the longest time, people would do things like do text only posts or an image. Because it kept on hearing there's certain better ways of getting more reach. The reality is, reach isn't the primary concern. It's the action that they perform once you reach them. So don't think, I need a video to do this. Instead, think I need an ad that does this the perform that helps me get more of these certain actions. So it may be a video, maybe something else. So think about what is the best format to help accomplish that goal. So typically, this isn't 100% all cases don't write into me angry saying I've used videos and they got me more traffic than links. I get it. They're always exceptions. But simplify this as much as possible. Links are the best at driving traffic. That's what they're for. You're not going to get a lot of comments and likes and shares and stuff on a link. Do you know why? Because once they click on it, they go to your website. They're not on Facebook anymore. Images are usually really good for just some kind of a simple engagement. You can get across a really simple message. People really like it. They can share it, comment on it, like whatever. And images are usually good for that. It's usually really light engagement though. Not necessarily getting you a sale or something.

## [03:00 - 05:16]

Videos are good. Now this is going to be kind of earth shattering too. For getting someone to watch. So if you use a video, you've got to have something that grabs their attention. It tells a story. There's a bunch of information here that needs to keep their attention for more than just a couple seconds. Which is all you need from an image or even from a link in a lot of cases. So you're trying to give them more information. Instant experience is good for a multi-format story. So if you're not familiar with instant experience, it redirects them after you click. But it stays within a Facebook experience. But you can basically build this out with different components. Like video and imagery and links and buttons. It's almost like building a landing page on your site. But it's within Facebook. Carousel, collections. It's good for just giving people options. It could be options. Just more information about something and multiple images. So something to keep in mind again. Everything has its own purpose. So if you're looking for conversion, oftentimes video is not going to do that. Or just using a static image. Won't do that. Because a video is best for getting someone to watch something. They're not necessarily going to then click it and go to your site and convert. So keep that in mind. Don't expect a link. All that said, just like I said a moment ago, don't expect a link to provide good engagement. Because you're driving them away from Facebook. That's where you want them to do everything.
